file_name: Prasad et al. - 2023 - ADTBO Aquila driving training-based optimization with deep learning for skin cancer detection.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning and optimization algorithms for skin cancer detection. Specifically, it proposes the ADTBO-SqueezeNet model, which combines the Aquila Driving Training-Based Optimization (ADTBO) algorithm with SqueezeNet for classification, and a Minkowski-based dual network (PsiNet and Deep Joint segmentation) for skin lesion segmentation.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'RESEARCH ARTICLE' on page 809. It presents original experimental work, including the proposal of a new ADTBO-SqueezeNet model, the use of a specific dataset (Skin Cancer: Malignant vs. Benign), and the reporting of new performance metrics (accuracy 0.976, specificity 0.984, sensitivity 0.987) and segmentation accuracy (0.948).

title: ADTBO: Aquila driving training-based optimization with deep learning for skin cancer detection
brief_summary: This study proposes an automatic skin cancer detection technique called ADTBO-SqueezeNet, which combines the Aquila Driving Training-Based Optimization (ADTBO) algorithm with SqueezeNet for classification. Skin lesion segmentation is performed using a Minkowski-based dual network comprising PsiNet and Deep Joint segmentation. The model was evaluated on the Skin Cancer: Malignant vs. Benign dataset, utilizing data augmentation and feature extraction to enhance detection effectiveness. The ADTBO-SqueezeNet achieved an accuracy of 0.976, specificity of 0.984, and sensitivity of 0.987. Additionally, the segmentation accuracy achieved by the Minkowski-based Dual Network was 0.948. The results indicate that the proposed method outperforms traditional schemes such as Lightweight CNN, ROI-based TL, RCNN, and DE-ANN.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study uses the 'Skin Cancer: Malignant vs. Benign' dataset. The text identifies Melanoma as the harmful type of skin cancer and the prevalent category. The classification task distinguishes between malignant and benign cases.

The study uses the 'Skin Cancer: Malignant vs. Benign' dataset containing balanced images of benign and malignant skin moles. While the paper lists Benign Keratosis, Melanocytic Nevus, Dermatofibroma, and Vascular lesions as types of skin conditions in the introduction, the experimental classification is binary (Malignant vs. Benign) using the specified dataset.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic category (Malignant vs. Benign skin cancer)
all_task_types.details: The paper proposes ADTBO-SqueezeNet for skin cancer detection, performing binary classification between malignant and benign skin moles using the 'Skin Cancer: Malignant vs. Benign' dataset. Performance metrics include accuracy (0.976), specificity (0.984), and sensitivity (0.987).

The paper employs a Minkowski-based dual network comprising PsiNet and Deep Joint segmentation to separate the exaggerated region (lesion) from the processed images. Segmentation accuracy achieved was 0.948.

datasets_used.name: Skin Cancer: Malignant vs. Benign
datasets_used.description: A balanced image dataset containing skin moles of both benign and malignant types. The dataset comprises two folders, with each folder containing 1800 images.

use.training: Used for training the ADTBO-SqueezeNet model with varying training data percentages (learning sets) and k-fold values.
use.validation: Used for validation during the training process via k-fold cross-validation.
use.testing: Used for testing and performance evaluation (accuracy, specificity, sensitivity) of the proposed model.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used for validation during the training process of the ADTBO-SqueezeNet model.
Used as the fitness function to determine the best solution during the optimization of SqueezeNet parameters.

statistical_methods_used.method: Statistical feature extraction (Mean, Kurtosis, Variance, Skewness)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used to extract features from the computed texture vector for classification.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, specificity, sensitivity, and segmentation accuracy, with comparisons made based on metric values rather than statistical significance.

Skin Cancer: Malignant vs. Benign.notes: The ADTBO-SqueezeNet model achieved its best performance with an accuracy of 0.976, specificity of 0.984, and sensitivity of 0.987 when using a k-fold value of 9. It outperformed comparative methods including Lightweight CNN, ROI-based TL, RCNN, optimized Deep CNN, and DE-ANN. Performance varied based on learning set percentage (e.g., 0.954 accuracy at 90% learning set) and epoch count.

Segmentation Analysis.notes: The Minkowski-based Dual Network achieved the highest segmentation accuracy of 0.948, outperforming U-Net, PsiNet, DeepJoint, and PSPNet.

architecture_used.main_model: ADTBO-SqueezeNet (SqueezeNet optimized by Aquila Driving Training-Based Optimization) and Minkowski-based Dual Network (PsiNet and Deep Joint segmentation)

classification_model.name: SqueezeNet
classification_model.structure: Divided into three stages: conv layer, final conv layer, and eight Fire modules.

optimization_algorithm.name: ADTBO
optimization_algorithm.purpose: Optimization of SqueezeNet parameters (tunable sizes in Fire modules)

segmentation_model.name: Minkowski-based Dual Network
segmentation_model.psinet_structure: Encoder-decoder network similar to UNet with one contracting encoder and three expansive decoders. Shape resembles numerical symbol 'c'. Encoder path uses repeated downsampling with ReLU activation, convolution, and stride. Decoder parts are symmetrical to encoder with feature concatenation.
segmentation_model.psinet_decoder_channels: One channel in distance decoder part, equivalent to input classes count in remaining two parts.

training_details.optimizer: ADTBO (Hybrid of Aquila Optimizer and Driving Training-Based optimization)

loss_function.classification: Mean Squared Error (MSE) used as fitness function: F = (1/B) * sum((Z* - Zy)^2)
loss_function.segmentation: Total loss lt = A1*lm + A2*lc + A3*ld (Mask loss, contour loss, distance loss)

training_details.data_augmentation: Data augmentation and feature extraction (Statistical features: Mean, Kurtosis, Variance, Skewness; Texture features: GLCM-based Energy, Entropy, ASM) were completed to enhance effectiveness.

preprocessing_techniques: Adaptive Median Filter for noise removal
Skin lesion segmentation using Minkowski-based dual networks (PsiNet and Deep Joint segmentation)
Data augmentation including geometric transformations (shifting, scaling, center cropping, rotation, flipping) and color space transformations (random brightness)
Feature extraction (Statistical features: Mean, Kurtosis, Variance, Skewness; Texture features: GLCM-based Energy, Entropy, ASM; CNN features; HoG features)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Geometric transformation involving left, right, up, and bottom shifting to remove image bias.
Geometric transformation to measure the actual size of the image.
Geometric transformation involving cropping the central part of the image.

Geometric transformation revolving the image to the left or right. Specific meaningful angles reported are 1 to 20 degrees or -1 to -20 degrees; angles increasing beyond this range may cause label loss.
Geometric transformation flipping the image in horizontal, vertical, or both directions.
Color space transformation used to progress the illumination of the image.

conclusions: The proposed ADTBO-SqueezeNet model, which combines the Aquila Driving Training-Based Optimization (ADTBO) algorithm with SqueezeNet, achieved high performance in skin cancer detection with an accuracy of 0.976, specificity of 0.984, and sensitivity of 0.987.
The Minkowski-based dual network, comprising PsiNet and Deep Joint segmentation, achieved a segmentation accuracy of 0.948.

The ADTBO-SqueezeNet model outperformed comparative methods including Lightweight CNN, ROI-based TL, RCNN, optimized Deep CNN, DE-ANN, ROA + SqueezeNet, WOA + SqueezeNet, AO + SqueezeNet, and DTBO + SqueezeNet.
The Minkowski-based dual network outperformed other segmentation models such as U-Net, PsiNet, DeepJoint, and PSPNet.
Data augmentation and feature extraction were effective in enhancing the detection performance of the proposed method.
The study utilized the 'Skin Cancer: Malignant vs. Benign' dataset, which contains balanced images of benign and malignant skin moles.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the 'Skin Cancer: Malignant vs. Benign' dataset, which contains only 3,600 balanced images (1,800 per class), limiting the generalizability of the findings to larger or more diverse clinical populations.

The paper explicitly acknowledges that future work is required to validate the model's accuracy across diverse ethnicities and skin categories, indicating a current lack of external validation on heterogeneous datasets.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed; performance comparisons were based solely on metric values rather than statistical significance.

The dataset is described as 'balanced,' which may not reflect real-world clinical prevalence rates where malignant cases are typically rarer than benign ones, potentially inflating perceived clinical utility.
The proposed method is a computational simulation without evidence of clinical deployment, prospective trials, or comparison against dermatologist performance in a clinical setting.

The segmentation component (Minkowski-based Dual Network) and classification component (ADTBO-SqueezeNet) are evaluated separately; the end-to-end performance of the combined pipeline is not reported.

The optimization algorithm (ADTBO) is a novel hybrid metaheuristic; its computational cost and training time are not compared against standard optimizers or manual hyperparameter tuning, which is a concern for practical deployment.
